sentences.text: Yes.
He explained to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how to live and what not to do, and very fundamental things.
But it's very sad to know that you will never see a person again.
And we both know when I come back, you know, out of six weeks [INAUDIBLE] in quarantine, he'll be dead.
I think that was one of the saddest things in my life.
Very fundamental.
If you have to step over people, be sure not-- not to step on their back.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yeah?
And how to treat people, and how never to let them know when you're afraid.
And you know, things you-- you tell a young person.
Oh, yes.
Very much so.
Very often I think about them.
And if in doubt, you know, I always think, what would grandpa say?
[LAUGHS] I still do.
Yeah.
She-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] she lived in some place in another barrack.
And then mother was notified that she died.
And then she buried her.
It was very sad when I died-- when he died, and I was in quarantine, my mother came to see me, to tell me that grandfather had died.
And just at that moment, all the coffins were being carried past where I was looking out of the window.
And she said, he's in one of them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Terrible.
I mean this is very sad.
There was a kind of a funeral, you know, but I don't remember much.
I know that my grandmother was-- her coffin opened inadvertently, and an arm came out.
It was also [INAUDIBLE].
But I think then, later on, they were, you know, cremated and got into urns.
A small-- a small thing was said.
That's all.

How people in the whole, or as a whole, are capable of being alive, staying alive, being, quote-unquote, happy, knowing that every day can be-- next day can be doomsday, but to enjoy that you are alive-- alive today.
And it's very, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] very interesting to see that in retrospect.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Because some of the things which you are confronted with were very sad, be it the dying people, be it the starving children, or people who suddenly lost their minds.
Not the hard work.
I mean, everybody has to work for a living.
But that was the saddest thing.
No.
No.
I knew I wanted to be on my birthday on the palms.
That must have been in '44, in June.
They were from Denmark and Swe-- and Sweden, and Switzerland.
And the whole camp was being, like, beautified for these people.
I don't know how many there were, maybe eight or 10.
And suddenly there were flowers, and there were better food.
And because you never know when they would be coming.
And they were just officers from these particular committees.
And I was confronted with them while I was working in the post office.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And for some reason-- I don't know, probably because I wore something red, they asked me questions that wasn't scheduled.

And they kind of sat and stood-- half sat, half stood in front of me, and asked me all kinds of questions, which were [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] not leading-- leading questions, but you had to be very careful how to answer them.
And behind me was one of the SS guys, so I had to be sure to say the right thing because otherwise, I would be in the little prison.
And-- but before we started, they somehow let me know that one of them had, in their lapel, a camera, and another one had the microphone.
So whatever they asked-- if-- I had to say what the Germans wanted me to say, but my face would say something different.
So when they put the two and two together, they knew.
Well, it was always, did they have good food, or-- I can't remember all of it.
But, you know.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Very leading and non-leading questions, you know, one after the other.
They were very schooled and skilled people how to do it.
And you just answered them very quickly.
And just you moved your face and tell them, you know?
You couldn't say no.
You had to say, yes.
But you let them know.
The [INAUDIBLE] is called the [GERMAN] It was called the little prison.
I'm lucky I never saw it.
It was away from Theresienstadt, and it was a prison.
Is was called the [GERMAN], and luckily, I never saw it on the inside.
Yeah.
I registered all the packages and passes which came in for the inmates.
There were different rules for the Czech people, and different rules for the German people.
The Czech people could get rather large packages every three months.
They had to have a certain stamp on it.
And the Germans could send whenever they wanted to.
But the German's didn't have any food.
So the would-- maybe they would send some potatoes, or maybe egg powder, or something.
They didn't have it.
Do you?
I'm familiar with it, but tell it.
The King of Denmark decided that he would not have all the Jews be deported.
And he helped to falsify papers.
But there were a small percentage he could not falsify.
He had to produce so and so many of them.
And I think they were about 150 or 200.
And he bid them goodbye.
Among them, one man, his name was Pulano, and he was an officer of his inner circle.
And Pulano was not allowed to take any civilian clothes.
He had to come in a full uniform.
And when they appeared in Theresienstadt, and Pulano came out as an officer-- as a high officer-- the Germans had to salute him.
It was very strange.
Of course, he got some work clothes later.
And the King kept his promise that none of his people were deported to Auschwitz.
And he saw to that.
And each of them got a package each-- one month.
Each month they got one.
And I don't know how they were constructed, but in a small package was everything in it, what you needed for a month, between egg powder, and this and that, and the other.
They really kept them alive-- kept them alive.
Also, before the war ended, he sent his three-- two or three buses back to pick up his Jews, so that they should not get into the turmoil of the end of the war.
Oh, yes.
They had to be opened.
There was a law.
There was a [NON-ENGLISH], or whatever you call them.
[NON-ENGLISH] they were Czech police people or something.
And they had to open them proforma.
Sometimes they looked into it, sometimes not.
You had to show that you have-- that it's you, who it is, and that's it.
Oh, yes.
There was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Theresien money.
Of course.
Yes.
You also could buy something.
There were-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] there were little restaurants where you could buy, maybe, a watered down drink of coffee, or something.
Or-- food you couldn't buy.
But the money was-- was distributed.
No, you got it anyway.
No, no, no.
You got that money anyway whether you worked or not.
It was like a ration, almost.
Yeah.
It was organized entertainment.
There were musicals, operas.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There was a quartet-- string quartet.
There was piano recitals.
There was a man called Meyer.
He played an accordion.
And you knew when they were.
Somehow there was-- I don't know how it was publicized.
But you went to see it.
It didn't cost you anything.
No.
No.
It was all very entertaining and very-- the people who did it, they were artists.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So it was entertaining for the time it lasted.
It took your mind off.
I know there were some in '43.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then, 10 in '44.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I left Auschwitz in one of them, on the 10-- the 10 or 11, which went in October of '44.
By will.
I don't know whose will.
I have no idea.
I was selected, because I was a half-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE].
I didn't have a father, though I had a step-father.
You know.
Oh, yes.
There was a government.
And an [NON-ENGLISH], as it was called.
And they made the laws.
And they-- these laws had to be [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] adhered to.
Well, there was no police.
But, you know, when they say you had to be home at such and such time, you went.
You know.
No.
No, no, no, no, no.
For what?
You know.
Senseless.
October '44.
I was notified.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then, it was very sad, because, since I was working for the post office, I knew I could not give any protection to my mother or her husband anymore.
Because, since I worked at the post office, I gave-- I quote, end quote, gave protection.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so this is how I got to Auschwitz.
And then one transport later, my mother and her husband got to Auschwitz.
Yeah.
In Novem-- on a November day.
I think '43 or '44.
It was cold.
And each living person had to leave the ghetto, and to get to some fields.
And everybody had to go with the people they lived with.
And then somebody came along, really, and counted the people-- one, two, three, four, five.
They wanted to know how many people they had.
And it took a day and a bit.
No, a day.
They started early in the morning, and then they counted.
And then they recounted.
And it was very dark when we got home.
But the bad part was, it was-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] you could only go through one person to get back into it again.
It was like a gate.
Can you imagine 70,000 people getting, one by one, through a gate?
Panic.
Absolute panic.
We got hungry.
We didn't get any food that day.
But I don't know.
They counted the people.
Even those who were, you know, in the hospital had to be carried out.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Next day was a normal life again.
Theresienstadt?
No.
No.
Whatever you able to have.
Because, you know, when you-- you're in a place for that long time, and you had very little clothes left.
So we came with a suitcase or two.
I mean, there was nothing much you could wear, because there was nothing left.
Oh, yes.
Very much.
Black market was-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I don't think there's any black market in the world which could top black market in Theresienstadt.
The [INAUDIBLE] would bring in whatever people wanted from them, or ask them for.
And they would bring it in.
How they brought in sometimes you wouldn't-- you know, on themselves-- or how they did it.
But everything was to be had.
And the [INAUDIBLE] were doing it.
I don't know.
I never needed anything badly enough.
But they-- I don't think they took too much-- too much for bringing it in.
But it was black market.
Be it a pair of shoes, or a suit, or glasses, or medication.
I know medication I once got for my-- for my stepfather.
No.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What I know [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] is they did business for the people in Theresienstadt on their day off.
And they met somehow in the ghetto, then, and concluded their business.
But they never charged much, the [INAUDIBLE].
There was-- they had the idea that they should be nice to the people in Theresienstadt.
An example is that sometimes the females had to go to the fields and work there.
And everybody wanted to go to the fields, which only a few people could do.
But as you left at one side-- one gate, there was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a mailbox.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was an unwritten rule that people could drop their letters into this mailbox, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just as they are.
And the postal offices would then forward the letters.
And the Germans very soon realized that, and they wanted to have this mailbox removed, and whatever.
But they couldn't do it.
And then they told the [INAUDIBLE] to take the people this way to the fields, and not that way.
But they wouldn't do it.
Everybody came there and dropped their mail.
And the Czech-- Czech mail officers would always deliver each letter.
And once you were on the fields, and let's say there were 25 females, then the [INAUDIBLE] would blow the whistle.
That would mean anybody can come.
So friends from the Czech-- from the Czech people, would come to the fields, have also something around their head, see their friends, and they would blow-- blow the whistle twice.
It means, get lost.
The Germans are coming.
And these people brought things in.
Cattle cars.
Cattle cars.
Cattle car is nothing.
Four walls and a roof.
And you put 50 people in there, either they sit or they stand, or whatever.
End of it.
Two days.
Mm-hm.
I knew very well.
Yeah.
While I was still in Hamburg, I had a terrible-- I had a dream.
And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I remember that I went downstairs to my mother, trying to explain this dream to her.
But I had to draw it.
It was something like a tower made out of wood, and an awful lot of wire, barbed wire around, and guns.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And it was a terrible dream, and it took a long time for me to recuperate from that dream.
So my mother, you know, ripped it apart, and we put fire to it, you know, put a match to it.
And she said, it's a terrible dream.
But maybe something happen-- will happen later in life.
And you'd think this was a warning.
So I figured, well, maybe this is where the warning comes into play.
I was waiting for it.
And when I got there, and the doors open, that was it.
So I was not so taken aback.
Because I had seen it already before.
I dreamt it.
And It was terrible.
People were crying and lamenting, and they didn't want to get out of these cattle cars.
And it was pitiful.
And there was our friend, Dr.
Mengele, standing, in his normal way, to the right and to the left, one of his assistants.
And he put his hands, similar to [INAUDIBLE], like this.
And would have a thumb going this side, that, and this.
This way means the other side.
In Czech, [CZECH], and this one is to stay alive.
We're taken off the train, yeah.
We lined up.
Well, I was given a wink to do-- [NON-ENGLISH], but I turned around.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he kind of looked at me and asked me to come back.
He didn't ask.
He shouted, you come back, you brat.
How dare you to turn around, et cetera, et cetera.
These things what you hear from people.
Why did you turn around?
And I said, excuse me, but you're very good-looking.
And because you're so good-looking, I turned around.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he kind of smirked, and pushed-- had me pushed to that side.
And that's-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that was-- the other side was the one where the people were living, you know, the 200 females out of this transport.
And the transport was 1,500 people.
And the 50 men they should get out of it, and 200 females.
And I was one from the 200 females.
Then we got into the normal camp there in Auschwitz, where these huge barracks with-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and there were sad people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No food.
Completely [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] old beyond their years, unable to stand straight anymore.
In between were the kapos, which you know what that is.
And the kapos were very, very harsh, and hated everybody except the kapos.
And they would hit you and [INAUDIBLE] with whips, and whatever have you.
They really had all the power there.
And you had to clean up.
There was only dirt, but you had to clean up the dirt.
We got very little food.
And there was very little way to sleep, except sideways on these [? bridges-- ?] not longways, sideways.
And you had an Appel.
In other words, you had to be counted three, four times a day.
And it was awful.
Many people died.
When we got there, some-- some-- you know, some corpses were lying around, but nobody paid attention.
Water soup.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What was in the soup, I have no idea.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Maybe you got a little piece of bread, or-- yeah.
But that's all.
To stay alive.
Try not to be hit by the kapos.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Try not to get too close to people who you knew will die at any minute.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know.
Just something general-- I have to stay alive.
How do I do it?
I have to stay alive.
I want to see the palms on my birthday.
And that's all.
You see what's going on.
You hear what's going on.
But you cannot allow it to touch you.
Because the minute it touches you, it affects you.
